# Developmental Disorders

## Make an Appointment

## Breadcrumb

Developmental disorder or developmental disability means a severe, chronic disability of an individual who has a mental or physical impairment by the age of 22 which is likely to continue indefinitely and results in substantial functional limitations in three or more areas of major life activity.

The term "developmental disorder" or "developmental disability" means a severe, chronic disability of an individual that:

*\*As defined by the Developmental Disabilities Assistance and Bill of Rights Act of 2000, Public Law 106-402.*

## Developmental Delay:

Developmental delay is defined as failure to meet expected developmental milestones in one or more of the following areas: physical, social, emotional, intellectual, speech and language and/or adaptive development (sometimes called self-help skills, which include dressing, toileting, feeding, etc).

These delays are diagnosed when a child performs approximately 25 to 30 percent below age norms in one or more of these areas (with adjustment for prematurity in affected children). Progress occurs at a slower than expected rate following the anticipated sequence. Various medical and environmental causes exist.

Some examples of physical or mental disorders likely to result in delayed development are:

The verification of delay is obtained through an evaluation process, which includes at least three of the following: informed clinical opinion to include observational assessment, standardized development test(s), developmental inventory, behavioral checklist, adaptive behavior measure and parent interview. Developmental delay can occur temporarily, or it can be long-term and never fully resolve.

# What are the Five Major Developmental Disabilities?

![Charlie Health Logo](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20viewBox='0%200%20500%20500'%3E%3C/svg%3E)

This Developmental Disabilities Month, we explore the most common types of developmental disabilities.

[Learn more about our Clinical Review Process](https://www.charliehealth.com/clinical-content-advisory-council)

Table of Contents

### Personalized intensive treatment from home

Ready to start healing?

## What are developmental disabilities?

Developmental disabilities, also known as developmental disorders, are a set of conditions that occur in early childhood and affect a person’s mental and physical growth. These disabilities can affect an individual’s ability to learn, communicate, and perform daily tasks. Understanding developmental disabilities is crucial to provide support and accommodations for individuals with these conditions.

## What are the 5 most common developmental disabilities?

### 1. Autism spectrum disorder

Autism spectrum disorder (ASD) is a neurodevelopmental condition that affects an individual’s ability to communicate, socialize, and interact with others. It is diagnosed when an individual exhibits persistent deficits in social communication and interaction, as well as restricted and repetitive patterns of behavior, interests, or activities.

Common characteristics of autism spectrum disorder include difficulties with social communication, such as eye contact, gestures, and facial expressions. Individuals with ASD may have a limited range of interests and activities and engage in repetitive behaviors, such as hand-flapping or lining up toys. They may also have difficulty with sensory processing and experience heightened sensitivity to certain sounds, textures, or smells.

ASD is a spectrum disorder, meaning that it affects individuals to varying degrees. Some individuals with ASD may have relatively mild symptoms and require minimal support, while others may require significant support and accommodations. ASD is linked to neurodivergence, and while there isn’t a one-size-fits-all approach to supporting neurodivergent people, seeking [neurodivergence-informed or affirming therapy](https://www.charliehealth.com/areas-of-care/neurodivergence) can be one way for people with ASD to receive support.

### 2. Cerebral palsy

Cerebral palsy is a group of disorders that affect an individual’s movement and coordination. It is caused by damage to the brain before, during, or after birth and is characterized by muscle weakness, spasticity, and difficulties with balance and coordination.

Common causes of cerebral palsy include brain damage or injury before or during birth, such as oxygen deprivation or infection. Premature birth and low birth weight are also risk factors for cerebral palsy.

Individuals with cerebral palsy may have difficulties with movement and coordination, including walking, sitting, and standing. They may also experience muscle stiffness or spasticity, which can affect their ability to perform daily tasks.

![Charlie Health shield logo](https://www.charliehealth.com/wp-content/themes/charliehealth/resources/images/logos/shield-darkest-blue.svg)

Looking for personalized neurodivergent therapy?

Learn if virtual IOP is the right fit for your mental health needs today.

### 3. Down syndrome

Down syndrome is a genetic condition that occurs when an individual has an extra copy of chromosome 21. It is characterized by cognitive delays, physical characteristics, and an increased risk of certain medical conditions.

Common risk factors for Down syndrome include advanced maternal age and a family history of the condition. Individuals with Down syndrome may have cognitive delays and difficulties with communication, social interaction, and daily living skills. They may also have physical characteristics, such as almond-shaped eyes, a small head, and a flattened facial profile.

### 4. Fetal alcohol spectrum disorders

Fetal alcohol spectrum disorders (FASD) are a group of conditions that occur when an individual is exposed to alcohol in the womb. It is characterized by cognitive and behavioral problems, as well as physical abnormalities. There are several types of FASD, including Fetal Alcohol Syndrome (FAS), Partial Fetal Alcohol Syndrome (pFAS), Alcohol-Related Neurodevelopmental Disorder (ARND), and Alcohol-Related Birth Defects (ARBD).

The most significant risk factor for FASD is a mother’s alcohol consumption during pregnancy. Individuals with FASD may have cognitive delays, difficulties with communication and social interaction, and behavioral problems such as impulsivity and hyperactivity. They may also have physical characteristics such as a small head, low birth weight, and facial abnormalities.

### 5. Intellectual disabilities

Individuals with intellectual disabilities typically experience significant limitations in intellectual functioning and adaptive behavior, which can affect their ability to learn, communicate, and perform daily activities. The condition specifically impacts cognitive abilities and is typically characterized by its onset during childhood.

## What’s the difference between developmental and intellectual disabilities?

Developmental disabilities and intellectual disabilities are two distinct but often overlapping categories of conditions that can affect individuals throughout their lives.

Developmental disabilities refer to a group of conditions that are usually present at birth or emerge during early childhood and can impact a child’s physical, cognitive, or behavioral abilities. The main types of developmental disabilities include autism spectrum disorder, cerebral palsy, fetal alcohol spectrum disorders, Down syndrome, and [attention deficit hyperactivity disorder](https://www.charliehealth.com/post/tips-for-young-people-with-adhd) (ADHD).

On the other hand, intellectual disabilities specifically affect an individual’s intellectual or cognitive abilities and are usually characterized by significant limitations in intellectual functioning and adaptive behavior. These limitations can affect an individual’s ability to learn, communicate, and perform daily activities, and typically become apparent during childhood.

It’s important to note that not everyone with a developmental disability will have an intellectual disability, and not all individuals with an intellectual disability will have a developmental disability. However, there can be significant overlap between the two categories, as many developmental disabilities can impact cognitive functioning and vice versa.

![Mother reading a story to her daughter in a library](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20viewBox='0%200%20800%20350'%3E%3C/svg%3E)![Mother reading a story to her daughter in a library](https://www.charliehealth.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/06/6414b7e72476f4ae2f15ba3b_5developmentaldisabilitiesInText-1.webp)
![Mother reading a story to her daughter in a library](https://www.charliehealth.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/06/6414b7e72476f4ae2f15ba3b_5developmentaldisabilitiesInText-1.webp)

## What are some risk factors for developmental disabilities?

There are many risk factors that can contribute to the emergence of developmental disorders. Some of the most common risk factors include:

### Genetic and chromosomal disorders

Certain genetic and chromosomal disorders, such as Down syndrome and Fragile X syndrome, can increase the risk of developmental disabilities.

### Premature birth and low birth weight

Babies who are born prematurely or with a low birth weight are at a higher risk for developmental disabilities.

### Prenatal exposure to toxins

Exposure to toxins during pregnancy, such as alcohol, tobacco, and drugs, can increase the risk of developmental disabilities.

### Maternal infections during pregnancy

Certain infections, such as rubella, cytomegalovirus, and toxoplasmosis, can increase the risk of developmental disabilities in babies.

### Brain injury or trauma

Traumatic brain injury, infections, and other injuries to the brain can increase the risk of developmental disabilities.

### Lack of access to healthcare and early intervention services

Children who do not receive timely healthcare and early intervention services are at a higher risk of developing developmental disabilities.

### Poverty and social disadvantage

Children from low-income families and disadvantaged backgrounds are at a higher risk of developmental disabilities due to lack of resources, exposure to toxins, and other environmental factors.

Having one or more risk factors does not necessarily mean that an individual will develop a developmental disorder. Many factors can influence a person’s development, and supportive environments and interventions can often help individuals with developmental disabilities reach their full potential.

## How are developmental disabilities tied to mental health?

Developmental disabilities can be tied to mental health in several ways. Individuals with developmental disabilities are more likely to experience mental health challenges including [anxiety](https://www.charliehealth.com/areas-of-care/anxiety), [depression](https://www.charliehealth.com/areas-of-care/depression), and behavioral disorders. These challenges can be related to their disability or to the challenges they face in daily life, such as [social isolation](https://www.charliehealth.com/post/isolations-silent-role-in-the-teen-mental-health-crisis), communication difficulties, and difficulty accessing services.

The link between developmental disabilities and mental health can also be bidirectional, meaning that mental health challenges can contribute to the development or exacerbation of developmental disorders. For example, children who experience significant emotional trauma or neglect may be at higher risk for developmental delays and behavioral challenges.

Furthermore, individuals with developmental disorders often face significant barriers to accessing mental disorder services and support. This can include a lack of trained providers who are equipped to work with individuals with disabilities, financial barriers to accessing care, and communication challenges that make it difficult to express their needs and experiences.

It is important to recognize and address the mental health needs of individuals with developmental disabilities. This can involve providing targeted interventions and support, addressing the social determinants of health that can impact mental health outcomes, and ensuring that individuals with disabilities have access to inclusive and culturally responsive mental health services.

## Understanding developmental disabilities

Understanding developmental disabilities is crucial to providing support and accommodations for individuals with these conditions. Intellectual disability, autism spectrum disorder, cerebral palsy, down syndrome, and fetal alcohol spectrum disorders are just a few examples of the many developmental disabilities that affect individuals.

Each condition has its own set of challenges and characteristics, and it is essential to approach each individual with a personalized and supportive approach. Providing access to education, therapy, and resources can help individuals with developmental disabilities reach their full potential.

It is also important to promote awareness and acceptance of individuals with developmental disorders and to advocate for their rights and inclusion in society. By working together, we can create a more inclusive and supportive world for individuals with developmental disabilities.

## Seeking mental health support from Charlie Health

Our mental health providers have extensive experience working with teens and young adults from all walks of life. Charlie Health’s therapists practice neurodivergence-informed therapy and celebrate the unique differences that make you, you. At Charlie Health, every client is matched with a therapist who fits their specific needs, and will also be matched with a group of peers who are from similar backgrounds with similar struggles. You are not alone with your mental health challenges. If you or your child are struggling, [get help now](https://app.charliehealth.com/intake).

--- SOURCE 4: Developmental disorder ---
URL: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Developmental_disorder

SUMMARY:
There are several ways of using this term.( The most narrow concept is used in the category "Specific Disorders of Psychological Development" in the ICD-10.( These disorders comprise developmental language disorder, learning disorders, developmental coordination disorders, and autism spectrum disorders (ASD).( In broader definitions, attention deficit hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) is included, and the term used is neurodevelopmental disorders.( Yet others include antisocial behavior and schizophrenia that begins in childhood and continues through life.( However, these two latter conditions are not as stable as the other developmental disorders, and there is not the same evidence of a shared genetic liability.( There is no specific treatment for autism spectrum disorders, but there are several types of therapy effective in easing the symptoms of autism, such as Applied Behavior Analysis (ABA), Speech-language therapy, Occupational therapy or Sensory integration therapy._[citation needed_]

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Not to be confused with [Pervasive developmental disorder](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pervasive_developmental_disorder "Pervasive developmental disorder").

Medical condition

| Developmental disorder |
| --- |
| [Specialty](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Medical_specialty "Medical specialty") | [Psychiatry](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Psychiatry "Psychiatry") |

**Developmental disorders** comprise a group of [psychiatric](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Psychiatry "Psychiatry") conditions originating in childhood that involve serious impairment in different areas. There are several ways of using this term.[[1]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Developmental_disorder#cite_note-Rutter3233-1) The most narrow concept is used in the category "Specific Disorders of Psychological Development" in the [ICD-10](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ICD-10 "ICD-10").[[1]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Developmental_disorder#cite_note-Rutter3233-1) These disorders comprise [developmental language disorder](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Developmental_language_disorder "Developmental language disorder"), [learning disorders](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Learning_disorder "Learning disorder"), [developmental coordination disorders](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Developmental_coordination_disorder "Developmental coordination disorder"), and [autism spectrum](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Autism_spectrum "Autism spectrum") disorders (ASD).[[2]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Developmental_disorder#cite_note-2) In broader definitions, [attention deficit hyperactivity disorder](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Attention_deficit_hyperactivity_disorder "Attention deficit hyperactivity disorder") (ADHD) is included, and the term used is [neurodevelopmental disorders](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Neurodevelopmental_disorder "Neurodevelopmental disorder").[[1]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Developmental_disorder#cite_note-Rutter3233-1) Yet others include [antisocial behavior](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Antisocial_behavior "Antisocial behavior") and [schizophrenia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Schizophrenia "Schizophrenia") that begins in childhood and continues through life.[[1]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Developmental_disorder#cite_note-Rutter3233-1) However, these two latter conditions are not as stable as the other developmental disorders, and there is not the same evidence of a shared genetic liability.[[1]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Developmental_disorder#cite_note-Rutter3233-1)

Developmental disorders are present from early life onward. Most improve as the child grows older, but some entail impairments that continue throughout life. These disorders differ from [Pervasive developmental disorders](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pervasive_developmental_disorder "Pervasive developmental disorder") (PPD), which uniquely describe a group of five developmental diagnoses, one of which is autism spectrum disorders (ASD). Pervasive developmental disorders reference a limited number of conditions whereas development disorders are a broad network of social, communicative, physical, genetic, intellectual, behavioral, and language concerns and diagnoses.

Emergence
---------

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Developmental_disorder&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Emergence")]

Learning disabilities are often diagnosed when the children are young and just beginning school. Most learning disabilities are found under the age of 9.[[3]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Developmental_disorder#cite_note-3)

Young children with communication disorders may not speak at all, or may have a limited vocabulary for their age.[[4]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Developmental_disorder#cite_note-comm-4) Some children with communication disorders have difficulty understanding simple directions or cannot name objects.[[4]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Developmental_disorder#cite_note-comm-4) Most children with communication disorders can speak by the time they enter school, however, they continue to have problems with communication.[[4]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Developmental_disorder#cite_note-comm-4) School-aged children often have problems understanding and formulating words.[[4]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Developmental_disorder#cite_note-comm-4) Teens may have more difficulty with understanding or expressing abstract ideas.[[4]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Developmental_disorder#cite_note-comm-4)

### Causes

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Developmental_disorder&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Causes")]

The scientific study of the causes of developmental disorders involves many theories. Some of the major differences between these theories involves whether environment disrupts normal development, if abnormalities are pre-determined, or if they are products of human evolutionary history which become disorders in modern environments (_see [evolutionary psychiatry](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Evolutionary\_psychiatry "Evolutionary psychiatry")_).[[5]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Developmental_disorder#cite_note-Karmiloff_Annette-5) Normal development occurs with a combination of contributions from both the environment and [genetics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Genetics "Genetics"). The theories vary in the part each factor has to play in normal development, thus affecting how the abnormalities are caused.[[5]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Developmental_disorder#cite_note-Karmiloff_Annette-5)

One theory that supports environmental causes of developmental disorders involves stress in early childhood. Researcher and child psychiatrist [Bruce D. Perry, M.D., Ph.D](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bruce_D._Perry "Bruce D. Perry"), theorizes that developmental disorders can be caused by early childhood [traumatization](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Traumatic_memories "Traumatic memories").[[6]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Developmental_disorder#cite_note-Perry,_Bruce_D_2006,_p.2-6) In his works, he compares developmental disorders in traumatized children to adults with post-traumatic stress disorder, linking extreme environmental stress to the cause of developmental difficulties.[[6]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Developmental_disorder#cite_note-Perry,_Bruce_D_2006,_p.2-6) Other stress theories suggest that even small stresses can accumulate to result in [emotional](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Emotional_and_behavioral_disorders "Emotional and behavioral disorders"), behavioral, or [social disorders](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rebellion "Rebellion") in children.[[7]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Developmental_disorder#cite_note-7)

A 2017 study[[8]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Developmental_disorder#cite_note-8)[[9]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Developmental_disorder#cite_note-9) tested all 20,000 genes in about 4,300 families with children with rare developmental difficulties in the [UK](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom") and Ireland in order to identify if these difficulties had a genetic cause. They found 14 new developmental disorders caused by spontaneous genetic mutations not found in either parent (such as a fault in the [CDK13 gene](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/CDK13_(gene) "CDK13 (gene)")). They estimated that about one in 300 children are born with spontaneous genetic mutations associated with rare developmental disorders.[[10]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Developmental_disorder#cite_note-10)

Types
-----

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Developmental_disorder&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Types")]

### Autism spectrum disorder (ASD)

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Developmental_disorder&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Autism spectrum disorder (ASD)")]

#### Diagnosis

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Developmental_disorder&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Diagnosis")]

The first diagnosed case of ASD was published in 1943 by American psychiatrist [Leo Kanner](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Leo_Kanner "Leo Kanner"). There is a wide range of cases and severity to ASD so it is very hard to detect the first signs of ASD. A diagnosis of ASD can be made accurately before the child is 3 years old, but the diagnosis of ASD is not commonly confirmed until the child is somewhat older. The age of diagnosis can range from 9 months to 14 years, and the mean age is 4 years old in the USA.[[11]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Developmental_disorder#cite_note-11) On average each case of ASD is tested at three different diagnostic centers before confirmed. Early diagnosis of the disorder can diminish familial stress, speed up referral to special educational programs and influence family planning.[[12]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Developmental_disorder#cite_note-Dereu,_Mieke_2010-12) The occurrence of ASD in one child can increase the risk of the next child having ASD by 50 to 100 times.[_[citation needed](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation\_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")_]

#### Abnormalities in the brain

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Developmental_disorder&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Abnormalities in the brain")]

The cause of ASD is still uncertain. What is known is that a child with ASD has a pervasive problem with how the brain is wired. Genes related to neurotransmitter receptors (serotonin and gamma-aminobutyric acid [GABA]) and CNS structural control (_HOX_ genes) are found to be potential target genes that get affected in ASD.[[13]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Developmental_disorder#cite_note-msdmanuals.com-13) Autism spectrum disorder is a disorder of the many parts of the brain. Structural changes are observed in the cortex, which controls higher functions, sensation, muscle movements, and memory. Structural defects are seen in the cerebellum too, which affect the motor and communication skills.[[14]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Developmental_disorder#cite_note-14) Sometimes the left lobe of the brain is affected and this causes neuropsychological symptoms. The distribution of white matter, the nerve fibers that link diverse parts of the brain, is abnormal. The corpus callosum, the band of nerve fibers, that connects the left and right hemispheres of the brain also gets affected in ASD. A study also found that 33% of people who have AgCC (agenesis of the corpus callosum), a condition in which the corpus callosum is partially or completely absent, had scores higher than the autism screening cut-off.[[15]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Developmental_disorder#cite_note-15)

An ASD child's brain grows at a very rapid rate and is almost fully grown by the age of 10.[[12]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Developmental_disorder#cite_note-Dereu,_Mieke_2010-12) Recent fMRI studies have also found altered connectivity within the social brain areas due to ASD and may be related to the social impairments encountered in ASD.[[16]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Developmental_disorder#cite_note-Gotts_et_al-16)[[17]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Developmental_disorder#cite_note-Subbaraju_et_al-17)

#### Symptoms

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Developmental_disorder&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Symptoms")]

The symptoms have a wide range of severity. The symptoms of ASD can be broadly categorised[[13]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Developmental_disorder#cite_note-msdmanuals.com-13) as the following:

##### Persistent issues in social interactions and communications

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Developmental_disorder&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Persistent issues in social interactions and communications")]

These are predominantly seen by unresponsiveness in conversations, lesser emotional sharing, inability to initiate conversations, inability to interpret body language, avoidance of eye-contact and difficulty maintaining relationships.[_[citation needed](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation\_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")_]

##### Repetitive behavioral patterns

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Developmental_disorder&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Repetitive behavioral patterns")]

These patterns can be seen in the form of repeated movements of the hand or the phrases used while talking. A rigid adherence to schedules and inflexibility to adapt even if a minor change is made to their routine is also one of the behavioral symptoms of ASD. They could also display sensory patterns such as extreme aversion to certain odors or indifference to pain or temperature.[_[citation needed](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation\_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")_]

There are also different symptoms at different ages based on developmental milestones. Children between 0 and 36 months with ASD show a lack of eye contact, seem to be deaf, lack a social smile, do not like being touched or held, have unusual sensory behavior and show a lack of imitation. Children between 12 and 24 months with ASD show a lack of gestures, prefer to be alone, do not point to objects to indicate interest, are easily frustrated with challenges, and lack of functional play. And finally children between the ages of 24 and 36 months with ASD show a lack of symbolic play and an unusual interest in certain objects, or moving objects.[[12]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Developmental_disorder#cite_note-Dereu,_Mieke_2010-12)

#### Treatment

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Developmental_disorder&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Treatment")]

There is no specific treatment for [autism spectrum disorders](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Autism_Spectrum_Disorders "Autism Spectrum Disorders"), but there are several types of therapy effective in easing the symptoms of autism, such as [Applied Behavior Analysis (ABA)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Applied_behavior_analysis "Applied behavior analysis"), [Speech-language therapy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Speech-language_therapy "Speech-language therapy"), [Occupational therapy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Occupational_therapy "Occupational therapy") or [Sensory integration therapy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sensory_integration_therapy "Sensory integration therapy").[_[citation needed](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation\_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")_]

[Applied behavioral analysis](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Applied_behavioral_analysis "Applied behavioral analysis") (ABA) is considered the most effective therapy for [Autism spectrum disorders](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Autism_spectrum "Autism spectrum") by the [American Academy of Pediatrics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/American_Academy_of_Pediatrics "American Academy of Pediatrics").[[18]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Developmental_disorder#cite_note-18) ABA focuses on teaching adaptive behaviors like social skills, play skills, or communication skills[[19]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Developmental_disorder#cite_note-19)[[20]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Developmental_disorder#cite_note-Treating_adaptive_living_skills_of_persons_with_autism_using_applied_behavior_analysis:_A_review-20) and diminishing problematic behaviors like self-injury.[[21]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Developmental_disorder#cite_note-21) This is done by creating a specialized plan that uses behavioral therapy techniques, such as positive or negative reinforcement, to encourage or discourage certain behaviors over-time.[[22]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Developmental_disorder#cite_note-22)

[Occupational therapy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Occupational_therapy "Occupational therapy") helps autistic children and adults learn everyday skills that help them with daily tasks, such as personal hygiene and movement. These skills are then integrated into their home, school, and work environments. Therapists will oftentimes help patients learn to adapt their environment to their skill level.[[23]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Developmental_disorder#cite_note-23) This type of therapy could help autistic people become more engaged in their environment.[[24]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Developmental_disorder#cite_note-autismspeaks.org-24) An occupational therapist will create a plan based on the patient's needs and desires and work with them to achieve their set goals.[_[citation needed](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation\_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")_]

[Speech-language therapy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Speech-language_therapy "Speech-language therapy") can help those with autism who need to develop or improve communication skills. According to the organization Autism Speaks, “speech-language therapy is designed to coordinate the mechanics of speech with the meaning and social use of speech”.[[24]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Developmental_disorder#cite_note-autismspeaks.org-24) People with low-functioning autism may not be able to communicate with spoken words. Speech-language Pathologists (SLP) may teach someone how to communicate more effectively with others or work on starting to develop speech patterns.[[25]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Developmental_disorder#cite_note-25) The SLP will create a plan that focuses on what the child needs.

[Sensory integration therapy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sensory_integration_therapy "Sensory integration therapy") helps people with autism adapt to different kinds of sensory stimuli. Many children with autism can be oversensitive to certain stimuli, such as lights or sounds, causing them to overreact. Others may not react to certain stimuli, such as someone speaking to them.[[26]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Developmental_disorder#cite_note-26) Many types of therapy activities involve a form of play, such as using swings, toys and trampolines to help engage the patients with sensory stimuli.[[24]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Developmental_disorder#cite_note-autismspeaks.org-24) Therapists will create a plan that focuses on the type of stimulation the person needs integration with.[_[citation needed](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation\_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")_]

### Attention deficit hyperactivity disorder (ADHD)

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Developmental_disorder&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Attention deficit hyperactivity disorder (ADHD)")]

[Attention deficit hyperactivity disorder](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Attention_deficit_hyperactivity_disorder "Attention deficit hyperactivity disorder") is a [neurodevelopmental disorder](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Neurodevelopmental_disorder "Neurodevelopmental disorder") that occurs in early childhood. ADHD affects 8 to 11% of children in the school going age.[_[citation needed](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation\_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")_] ADHD is characterised by significant levels of hyperactivity, inattentiveness, and impulsiveness. There are three subtypes of ADHD: predominantly inattentive, predominantly hyperactive, and combined (which presents as both hyperactive and inattentive subtypes).[[27]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Developmental_disorder#cite_note-Tresco_Katy_E._2004-27) ADHD is twice as common in boys than girls but it is seen that the hyperactive/impulsive type is more common in boys while the inattentive type affects both sexes equally.[[28]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Developmental_disorder#cite_note-ReferenceA-28)

#### Symptoms

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Developmental_disorder&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Symptoms")]

Symptoms of ADHD include inattentiveness, impulsiveness, and hyperactivity. Many of the behaviors that are associated with ADHD include poor control over actions resulting in disruptive behavior and academic problems. Another area that is affected by these disorders is the social arena for the person with the disorder. Many children that have this disorder exhibit poor interpersonal relationships and struggle to fit in socially with their peers.[[27]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Developmental_disorder#cite_note-Tresco_Katy_E._2004-27) Behavioral study of these children can show a history of other symptoms such as temper tantrums, mood swings, sleep disturbances and aggressiveness.[[28]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Deve

Developmental disorders affect a child's physical, cognitive, language or behavioral development. They can impact everyday functioning and usually last

FULL CONTENT:
![MaineHealth Logo](https://assets.mainehealth.io/s3fs-public/config_pages/header/MaineHealth-RGB-Preferred-Logo-Digital.svg)

## Primary Navigation

# Developmental Disorders

If a child has a developmental delay, it is important to get help as soon as possible. Early diagnosis and treatment can have a significant impact on a child’s ability to learn new skills.

## What are developmental disorders?

Developmental disorders affect a child’s physical, cognitive, language or behavioral development. They can impact everyday functioning and usually last throughout a person’s lifetime. Developmental disorders can affect physical abilities, such as vision, and mental abilities, such as learning. Many of these conditions affect multiple body parts or systems. Developmental disorders can take a toll on an individual’s physical, intellectual and emotional growth.

## More about developmental disorders

## Developmental disorders diagnosis

Growth and development are tracked by a child’s parents and provider.  The child’s primary care provider will evaluate the child for developmental delays or problems and address any concerns. This is called developmental monitoring or surveillance. Any problems noticed during developmental monitoring should be followed up with developmental screening.

Developmental screening is a short test to tell if a child is learning basic skills when he or she should, or if there are delays.

## Developmental disorder causes

Most developmental disorders begin before a baby is born, but some can happen after birth because of injury, infection or other factors.

Many developmental disorders are believed to be caused by different factors. Some factors include:

Injury

Infection

Genetics

Exposure to high levels of environmental toxins (such as lead)

Use of alcohol or other substances during pregnancy

Developmental disorders can happen to anyone.

## Developmental disorder types

Some types of developmental disorders include:

[ADHD](/care-services/pediatric-care-child-health/pediatric-adhd)

[Autism spectrum disorder](/care-services/behavioral-health-care/autism-spectrum-disorder-asd-mainehealth-behavioral-health)

[Cerebral palsy](/care-services/pediatric-care-child-health/cerebral-palsy)

Hearing loss

Intellectual disability

[Learning disability](/care-services/pediatric-care-child-health/learning-disabilities)

Vision impairment

Some people may have one or more developmental disabilities at once.

## Developmental disorder treatment

Developmental delay is generally not curable, however it can be treated. Treatment programs may include:

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

enriched_points.expanded_content: Developmental disorders are conditions that emerge during childhood, affecting cognitive, physical, and emotional development, often leading to long-term challenges in learning and behavior.
These disorders significantly hinder a child's ability to learn, communicate, and socialize, affecting academic performance and interpersonal relationships throughout life.
Early diagnosis and intervention can greatly enhance developmental outcomes, helping children acquire essential skills and reducing the severity of challenges faced in later life.
Approximately 1 in 6 children in the U.S. is diagnosed with a developmental disorder, highlighting the critical need for awareness and accessible support services.
Intervention strategies include behavioral therapies, speech-language therapy, occupational therapy, and educational support, tailored to each child's unique needs to promote development.
